sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After RIGHT from rows [25,26] cols [39,43], it was blocked (stayed at [39,43]). But then DOWN worked and moved the blocks to rows [40,41] cols [29,33]. This is a big jump - from rows [25,26] cols [39,43] to rows [40,41] cols [29,33]. That's a jump of 15 rows down and 10 cols left!
This is strange. It seems like the blocks "teleported" or the level reset. Let me check if the level actually reset.
Wait, looking more carefully:
- After RIGHT (blocked): rows [25,26] cols [39,43]
- After DOWN: rows [40,41] cols [29,33]
- After DOWN (blocked): rows [40,41] cols [29,33]
The DOWN move from rows [25,26] cols [39,43] resulted in rows [40,41] cols [29,33]. This is a big jump. It seems like the blocks moved through a portal or the level has some special mechanic.
Actually, wait. Looking at the image, the blocks are back at the starting position (rows [40,41] cols [29,33]). This is the same position as when the level started. Did the level reset?
Let me check the step count: it went from 88 to 95, which is 7 steps. The level didn't reset, but the blocks moved to a different position.
Actually, I think what happened is that the blocks moved through a corridor that wraps around or has a special connection. From rows [25,26] cols [39,43], moving DOWN caused the blocks to appear at rows [40,41] cols [29,33].
This is a key insight! The maze has corridors that connect different parts of the level. From the upper-right area (rows [25,26] cols [39,43]), moving DOWN leads to the center area (rows [40,41] cols [29,33]).
So the path is:
- From center (rows [40,41] cols [29,33]), RIGHT → [34,38]
- From [34,38], UP → [35,36] → [30,31] → [25,26]
- From [25,26], RIGHT → [39,43]
- From [39,43], DOWN → [40,41] cols [29,33] (center)
This is a loop! I've been going in circles.
But wait, the white cross is at rows [46,47] cols [51,52]. How do I reach it?
Looking at the image again, the white cross is in a separate room on the right side. The gray structure has a boundary at cols [9,58], so the white cross at cols [51,52] is near the right edge.
Let me think about the maze layout differently. The gray structure (ID=2) has children [3, 5, 10, 11, 12, 13]:
- ID=3: Yellow square at rows [16,18] cols [15,17] (top-left room)
- ID=5: Black box at rows [39,45] cols [13,19] (middle-left room)
- ID=10: White cross at rows [46,47] cols [51,52] (bottom-right room)
- ID=11, 12: Light gray pixels near white cross
- ID=13: Yellow square at rows [51,53] cols [40,42] (bottom room)
The white cross (ID=10) is a child of the gray structure (ID=2), which means it's inside the playable area. But how do I reach it?
From the adjacency list:
- ID=2 (gray structure) is adjacent to ID=10 (white cross)
- This means the white cross is directly adjacent to the gray structure's boundary
Wait, but the white cross is at rows [46,47] cols [51,52], which is inside the gray structure's bounds (rows [5,54], cols [9,58]). So the white cross is inside the playable area.
Let me look at the image more carefully. The white cross is in a room on the right side of the gray structure. The blocks need to navigate through the corridors to reach that room.
From the current position (rows [40,41] cols [29,33]), I need to find a path to the white cross. Let me try:
1. RIGHT to [34,38]
2. UP to [35,36], [30,31], [25,26]
3. RIGHT to [39,43]
4. From [39,43], try DOWN again - but this led back to center
Wait, maybe I need to try a different path. From [39,43], instead of DOWN, try RIGHT or UP.
Actually, looking at the image, there's a corridor on the right side that leads down to the white cross room. From [39,43], I need to go RIGHT to reach that corridor.
But earlier, RIGHT from [39,43] was blocked. Let me try a different approach: from [39,43], try UP first, then RIGHT.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the movement. From rows [25,26] cols [39,43], DOWN moved to rows [40,41] cols [29,33]. This is a big jump, suggesting there's a vertical corridor that connects the upper-right area to the center area.
But the white cross is at rows [46,47] cols [51,52], which is below and to the right of the current position. Maybe I need to find a path that goes through the bottom-right area.
Let me try: from center (rows [40,41] cols [29,33]), go RIGHT to [34,38], then DOWN to see if there's a path to the bottom-right area.
Wait, earlier I tried DOWN from [34,38] and it worked to [35,36], [40,41]. But that's going back to the center. Let me try RIGHT from [34,38] instead.
Actually, I realize I need to map out the maze more carefully. Let me try a systematic exploration from the current position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: rows [40,41] cols [29,33] (center)
# White cross at rows [46,47] cols [51,52]
#
# The DOWN from [25,26] cols [39,43] teleported to [40,41] cols [29,33]
# This suggests the maze has special connections
#
# Let me try RIGHT from center, then DOWN to explore bottom path

predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT from center')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try DOWN from [34,38]
predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN')
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try RIGHT
predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try DOWN
predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN')
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try RIGHT
predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Check white cross
white_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W']
print(f"White nodes: {len(white_nodes)}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
